serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving of sauteed cabbage with sour sauce is approximately 200 g, equivalent to a side-dish plate portion as typically served in a Chinese restaurant.

nutrient_source.title: Chinese Cabbage (Napa), cooked, boiled, drained, without salt — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy; supplemented with stir-fried vegetable dish composition estimates from USDA Foundation Foods for oil and seasoning contribution
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base values derived from USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked napa cabbage (FDC ID 169975). Adjustments applied for stir-frying with vegetable oil (~5 g oil per 100 g dish), rice vinegar, soy sauce, sugar, and sesame oil contributions, consistent with standard Chinese stir-fried vegetable dish composition. Sodium elevated to reflect soy sauce addition. Energy recalculated from adjusted macros using Atwater factors.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Atkinson FS, Brand-Miller JC, Foster-Powell K, Buyken AE, Goletzke J; analogous estimate based on cooked cabbage and vinegar-dressed vegetable dish entries
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for 醋溜白菜. GI estimated at 32 based on cooked napa cabbage (GI ~32, low category) from the University of Sydney GI Database. Vinegar addition is documented in peer-reviewed literature (Johnston et al., 2004, JADA) to attenuate postprandial glycaemia, supporting a low GI classification. Glycemic load calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (32 × 13.6) / 100 = 4.4.

hypertension.guidance: Each 200 g serving contributes approximately 760 mg sodium, which represents about 33% of the WHO recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced soy sauce or low-sodium soy sauce when ordering this dish.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index and vinegar-mediated glycaemic attenuation
type2_diabetes.guidance: With a GI of approximately 32 and a glycemic load of 4.4 per serving, this dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes; the acetic acid in rice vinegar has been shown in peer-reviewed studies to reduce postprandial blood glucose response, making this a favourable vegetable side dish choice.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Low saturated fat, high dietary fibre, and vegetable-dominant composition
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The dish is low in saturated fat (0.6 g per 100 g) and provides dietary fibre and antioxidant vitamins from cabbage; however, the moderate sodium content from soy sauce warrants attention for individuals managing cardiovascular risk through sodium restriction.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Sauteed Cabbage with Sour Sauce (醋溜白菜, Cù liū báicài) is a classic Northern Chinese stir-fried vegetable dish originating from Beijing cuisine. The dish is prepared by stir-frying napa cabbage (Chinese cabbage) at high heat in vegetable oil with garlic and dried chilli, then finishing with a tangy sauce made from rice vinegar, soy sauce, sugar, sesame oil, and a cornstarch slurry to create a glossy, lightly thickened coating. The result is a dish with a crisp-tender texture, a bright sour-sweet flavour profile, and a mild heat from the chilli. It is a staple home-cooked dish in Beijing households and is widely served in Northern Chinese restaurants across China and in overseas Chinese communities including Singapore. Nutritionally, the dish is low in calories (approximately 72 kcal per 100 g), low in saturated fat, and provides dietary fibre, vitamin C, folate, and potassium from the cabbage. The sodium content is moderate due to soy sauce and warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is low (approximately 32), and the acetic acid from rice vinegar has been documented in clinical literature to attenuate postprandial blood glucose, making this dish appropriate for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The dish is vegetarian, vegan, and halal-suitable. It is typically served as a side dish alongside rice or steamed buns (mantou) in a shared Chinese meal.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for 醋溜白菜 was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values were adapted from USDA SR Legacy cooked napa cabbage data with calculated adjustments for stir-frying oil and soy sauce seasoning contributions. GI is an analogous estimate based on cooked cabbage GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, supported by peer-reviewed evidence on vinegar's glycaemic-attenuating effect. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted estimates; no exact database entry for 醋溜白菜 was identified in any approved primary source. Values are based on USDA SR Legacy cooked napa cabbage with calculated additions for stir-frying oil (~5 g per 100 g dish), soy sauce sodium contribution, and minor sugar addition. 2. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish containing no animal products; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central for cooked napa cabbage. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish with no vitamin D-containing ingredients; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as no hydrogenated fats are used in this dish; confirmed zero by formulation. cholesterol_mg is 0.0 as this is a fully plant-based dish; confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 3. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g values are estimated from the fatty acid profile of vegetable oil used in stir-frying. 4. GI of 32 is an analogous estimate based on cooked napa cabbage from the University of Sydney GI Database; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found. 5. The input food_group 'Oils, seasonings and condiments' has been corrected to 'Vegetables' as this is a vegetable-dominant stir-fried dish. 6. Serving size of 200 g is a standard restaurant side-dish portion estimate. 7. Glycemic load per serving = (32 × 13.6 g carbohydrate per 200 g serving) / 100 = 4.4.
